title: Add Multiples of Ten to Two-Digit Numbers
description: Add a multiple of 10 to a two-digit number by increasing the tens digit while keeping the ones digit the same.
condition: Expression of the form a two-digit number + multiple of 10 (e.g., 35 + 20).
response: Increase the tens place by the given tens and keep the ones digit unchanged.
label: Principle / Model
example: For $35 + 20$: $35 = 3$ tens $5$ ones. Adding $2$ tens gives $5$ tens $5$ ones = $55$.

title: Add Tens Then Ones Strategy
description: Add a multiple of ten first, then add the remaining ones to reach a target number.
condition: When adding a two-digit number by decomposing it into tens and ones (e.g., +14 as +10 and +4).
response: Add the tens first, then add the ones to find the total.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Used as a counting-up strategy to find differences.

title: Add Three Numbers Within 100
description: Compute the total of three addends within 100, often by grouping or sequential addition.
condition: Given three whole numbers within 100 to be combined into one total (e.g., a + b + c).
response: Add the three numbers to find the total.
label: Principle
notes: Often decomposed into two sequential addition steps.

title: Add Two-Digit Numbers with Composing a Ten
description: Add two two-digit numbers where the ones sum to 10 or more, requiring composing a new ten.
condition: Addition of two two-digit numbers where ones digits sum to ≥ 10 (e.g., 23 + 19).
response: Add ones to form a new ten, regroup to tens place, then add tens.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Also used when adding three numbers in Q6.

title: Compute Two-Digit Subtraction Within 100
description: Accurately compute the difference between two two-digit numbers within 100 (no regrouping).
condition: Given a subtraction expression with two two-digit numbers within 100 that does not require regrouping.
response: State the correct numerical difference.
label: Production / Skill
notes: Procedural fluency outcome of place-value reasoning.

title: Decompose One Ten into Ten Ones
description: Trade one ten for ten ones when there are not enough ones to subtract.
condition: In subtraction, the ones digit of the minuend is less than the ones digit of the subtrahend (e.g., 45 − 18).
response: Decompose one ten into 10 ones and add them to the ones place before subtracting.
label: Principle
example: For $45 - 18$, rewrite $45$ as $3$ tens and $15$ ones, then subtract $8$ ones.

title: Decompose Subtrahend into Tens and Ones
description: Break apart a two-digit number into tens and ones (or further into parts) to make subtraction easier.
condition: Given a two-digit number to subtract (e.g., 28 or 17).
response: Rewrite the number as a sum of tens and ones (e.g., 28 = 20 + 8 or 20 + 5 + 3).
label: Principle

title: Decompose Two-Digit Number into Tens and Ones
description: Represent a two-digit number as a sum of tens and ones.
condition: Given any two-digit number (e.g., 65).
response: Express the number as tens and ones (e.g., 6 tens and 5 ones).
label: Concept

title: Evaluate and Match Expression to Target Value
description: Compute the value of expressions and determine which equal a specified target number.
condition: A set of numerical expressions and a target value (e.g., select expressions equal to 75).
response: Evaluate each expression and select those whose value matches the target.
label: Rule / Plan

title: Find Total of Multiple Quantities
description: Add three or more quantities to find a total.
condition: Table or list of three quantities requiring a total (e.g., 35 dogs, 19 rabbits, 27 cats).
response: Add all quantities accurately, composing tens as needed.
label: Principle / Model
notes: May involve multiple compositions of tens.

title: Interpret Base-Ten Block Subtraction Model
description: Identify the subtraction expression and result represented by base-ten blocks with some units crossed out.
condition: A base-ten block diagram showing tens and ones with some blocks crossed out.
response: State the subtraction equation represented and determine the remaining tens and ones.
label: Rule
example: A diagram showing 4 tens and 5 ones with 1 ten decomposed and 18 units crossed out represents $45 - 18 = 27$ (2 tens and 7 ones).
notes: Requires connecting visual model to symbolic subtraction.

title: Represent Add-To, Result Unknown
description: Translate an 'add to' story situation into an addition equation.
condition: Story problem describing an initial amount that increases (e.g., 40 stickers, gets 13 more).
response: Write and solve an addition equation representing total = start + change.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Jada has 40 stickers and gets 13 more → $40 + 13 = 53$.

title: Represent Part-Part-Whole Relationships with Equations
description: Express a part-part-whole situation as related addition and subtraction equations with an unknown in any position.
condition: Given a total and one or two parts in a part-part-whole context (e.g., tape diagram).
response: Write equivalent equations such as part + part = whole, whole − part = part, or ? + part = whole.
label: Principle
example: If total is 46 and one part is 29, write $? + 29 = 46$, $46 - 29 = ?$, or $46 - ? = 29$.

title: Represent Take-From, Result Unknown
description: Translate a 'take from' story situation into a subtraction equation.
condition: Story problem describing an initial amount that decreases (e.g., gave away 15).
response: Write and solve a subtraction equation representing remaining = start − change.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Jada had 53 stickers and gave away 15 → $53 - 15 = 38$.

title: Subtract Multiples of Ten from Two-Digit Numbers
description: Subtract a multiple of 10 from a two-digit number by decreasing the tens digit while keeping the ones digit the same.
condition: Expression of the form a two-digit number − multiple of 10 (e.g., 85 − 10).
response: Decrease the tens place by the given tens and keep the ones digit unchanged.
label: Principle / Model
example: For $85 - 10$: $8$ tens $5$ ones minus $1$ ten = $7$ tens $5$ ones = $75$.

title: Subtract Tens and Ones in Parts
description: Subtract tens first and then subtract ones to find the total difference.
condition: A two-digit subtraction problem where numbers are decomposed into tens and ones (e.g., 44 − 17).
response: Subtract the tens (e.g., 44 − 10 = 34) and then subtract the remaining ones (34 − 7 = 27).
label: Principle
notes: Relies on place-value structure and properties of operations.

title: Subtract Tens and Ones Without Regrouping
description: Subtract two-digit numbers by subtracting tens and ones separately when no regrouping is required.
condition: A subtraction problem within 100 where ones digit of minuend is greater than or equal to ones digit of subtrahend (e.g., 75 − 61).
response: Subtract tens from tens and ones from ones, then combine the results.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Restricted to cases not requiring composing or decomposing a ten.

title: Subtract Two-Digit Numbers with Decomposing a Ten
description: Subtract two-digit numbers when the ones digit of the minuend is smaller than the subtrahend, requiring decomposing a ten.
condition: Subtraction of two two-digit numbers where ones digit of minuend < ones digit of subtrahend (e.g., 53 − 17).
response: Decompose one ten into 10 ones, subtract ones, then subtract tens.
label: Principle / Model

title: Translate Tape Diagram to Equation
description: Map quantities and unknowns in a tape diagram to a matching numerical equation.
condition: Given a tape diagram showing parts and a whole with labeled quantities and/or unknowns.
response: Construct an equation that matches the relationships shown in the diagram.
label: Rule
example: A bar split into ? and 29 totaling 46 corresponds to $? + 29 = 46$.
notes: Requires aligning diagram structure with symbolic representation.

title: Use Addition–Subtraction Inverse Relationship
description: Use subtraction to find an unknown addend in an addition equation.
condition: An equation of the form $\square + a = b$.
response: Compute $b - a$ to find the unknown addend.
label: Principle / Model

title: Use Subtraction to Compare Quantities
description: Find how many fewer or more by subtracting the smaller quantity from the larger.
condition: Comparison question asking 'how many fewer/more' between two quantities.
response: Subtract smaller quantity from larger to find the difference.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Dogs: 35, Rabbits: 19. $35 - 19 = 16$, so 16 fewer rabbits than dogs.
notes: Interpretation of comparison language is conventional.
